author: Defence of the Nicene Definition. (De Decretis.)
book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)

classification.cautionReason: This work discusses heresy in order to refute it; do not classify it as heretical merely from its title.

title: Authorities in Support of the Council. Theognostus; Dionysius of Alexandria; Dionysius of Rome; Origen.

source.title: NPNF2-04. Athanasius: Select Works and Letters

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: This then is the sense in which they who met at Nicæa made use of these expressions.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: But next that they did not invent them for themselves (since this is one of their excuses), but spoke what they had received from their predecessors, proceed we to prove this also, to cut off even this excuse from them.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: Know then, O Arians, foes of Christ, that Theognostus, a learned man, did not decline the phrase ‘of the essence,’ for in the second book of his Hypotyposes, he writes thus of the Son:—

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: “The essence of the Son is not one procured from without, nor accruing out of nothing, but it sprang from the Father’s essence, as the radiance of light, as the vapour of water; for neither the radiance, nor the vapour, is the water itself or the sun itself, nor is it alien; but it is an effluence of the Father’s essence, which, however, suffers no partition.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: For as the sun remains the same, and is not impaired by the rays poured forth by it, so neither does the Father’s essence suffer change, though it has the Son as an Image of Itself.”

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: Theognostus then, after previously investigating in the way of an exercise, proceeds to lay down his sentiments in the foregoing words.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: Next, Dionysius, who was Bishop of Alexandria, upon his writing against Sabellius and expounding at large the Saviour’s Economy according to the flesh, and thence proving against the Sabellians that not the Father but His Word became flesh, as John has said, was suspected of saying that the Son as a thing made and originated, and not one in essence with the Father; on this he writes to his namesake Dionysius, Bishop of Rome, to allege in his defence that this was a slander upon him.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: And he assured him that he had not called the Son made, nay, did confess Him to be even one in essence.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: And his words ran thus:—

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: “And I have written in another letter a refutation of the false charge they bring against me, that I deny that Christ was one in essence with God.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: For though I say that I have not found this term anywhere in Holy Scripture, yet my remarks which follow, and which they have not noticed, are not inconsistent with that belief.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: For I instanced human birth as being evidently homogeneous, and I observed that undeniably parents differed from their children only in not being the same individuals, otherwise there could be neither parents nor children.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: And my letter, as I said before, owing to present circumstances I am unable to produce; or I would have sent you the very words I used, or rather a copy of it all, which, if I have an opportunity, I will do still.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: But I am sure from recollection that I adduced parallels of things kindred with each other; for instance, that a plant grown from seed or from root, was other than that from which it sprang, yet was altogether one in nature with it: and that a stream flowing from a fountain, gained a new name, for that neither the fountain was called stream, nor the stream fountain, and both existed, and the stream was the water from the fountain”

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: And that the Word of God is not a work or creature, but an offspring proper to the Father’s essence and indivisible, as the great Council wrote, here you may see in the words of Dionysius, Bishop of Rome, who, while writing against the Sabellians, thus inveighs against those who dared to say so:—

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: “Next, I may reasonably turn to those who divide and cut to pieces and destroy that most sacred doctrine of the Church of God, the Divine Monarchy, making it as it were three powers and partitive subsistences and god-heads three.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: I am told that some among you who are catechists and teachers of the Divine Word, take the lead in this tenet, who are diametrically opposed, so to speak, to Sabellius’s opinions; for he blasphemously says that the Son is the Father, and the Father the Son, but they in some sort preach three Gods, as dividing the sacred Monad into three subsistences foreign to each other and utterly separate.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: For it must needs be that with the God of the Universe, the Divine Word is united, and the Holy Ghost must repose and habitate in God; thus in one as in a summit, I mean the God of the Universe, must the Divine Triad be gathered up and brought together.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: For it is the doctrine of the presumptuous Marcion, to sever and divide the Divine Monarchy into three origins,—a devil’s teaching, not that of Christ’s true disciples and lovers of the Saviour’s lessons.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: For they know well that a Triad is preached by divine Scripture, but that neither Old Testament nor New preaches three Gods.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: Equally must one censure those who hold the Son to be a work, and consider that the Lord has come into being, as one of things which really came to be; whereas the divine oracles witness to a generation suitable to Him and becoming, but not to any fashioning or making.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: A blasphemy then is it, not ordinary, but even the highest, to say that the Lord is in any sort a handiwork.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: For if He came to be Son, once He was not; but He was always, if (that is) He be in the Father, as He says Himself, and if the Christ be Word and Wisdom and Power (which, as ye know, divine Scripture says), and these attributes be powers of God.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: If then the Son came into being, once these attributes were not; consequently there was a time, when God was without them; which is most absurd.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: And why say more on these points to you, men full of the Spirit and well aware of the absurdities which come to view from saying that the Son is a work?

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: Not attending, as I consider, to this circumstance, the authors of this opinion have entirely missed the truth, in explaining, contrary to the sense of divine and prophetic Scripture in the passage, the words, ‘The Lord created me a beginning of His ways unto His works.’

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: For the sense of ‘He created,’ as ye know, is not one, for we must understand ‘He created’ in this place, as ‘He set over the works made by Him,’ that is, ‘made by the Son Himself.’

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: And ‘He created’ here must not be taken for ‘made,’ for creating differs from making.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: ‘Is not He thy Father that hath bought thee? hath He not made thee and created thee?’says Moses in his great song in Deuteronomy.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: And one may say to them, O reckless men, is He a work, who is ‘the First-born of every creature, who is born from the womb before the morning star,’ who said, as Wisdom, ‘Before all the hills He begets me?’

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: And in many passages of the divine oracles is the Son said to have been generated, but nowhere to have come into being; which manifestly convicts those of misconception about the Lord’s generation, who presume to call His divine and ineffable generation a making.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: Neither then may we divide into three Godheads the wonderful and divine Monad; nor disparage with the name of ‘work’ the dignity and exceeding majesty of the Lord; but we must believe in God the Father Almighty, and in Christ Jesus His Son, and in the Holy Ghost, and hold that to the God of the universe the Word is united.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: For ‘I,’ says He, ‘and the Father are one;’ and, ‘I in the Father and the Father in Me.’

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: For thus both the Divine Triad, and the holy preaching of the Monarchy, will be preserved.”

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: And concerning the everlasting co-existence of the Word with the Father, and that He is not of another essence or subsistence, but proper to the Father’s, as the Bishops in the Council said, you may hear again from the labour-loving Origen also.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: For what he has written as if inquiring and by way of exercise, that let no one take as expressive of his own sentiments, but of parties who are contending in investigation, but what he definitely declares, that is the sentiment of the labour-loving man.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: After his prolusions then (so to speak) against the heretics, straightway he introduces his personal belief, thus:—

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: “If there be an Image of the Invisible God, it is an invisible Image; nay, I will be bold to add, that, as being the likeness of the Father, never was it not.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: For when was that God, who, according to John, is called Light (for ‘God is Light’), without a radiance of His proper glory, that a man should presume to assert the Son’s origin of existence, as if before He was not?

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: But when was not that Image of the Father’s Ineffable and Nameless and Unutterable subsistence, that Expression and Word, and He that knows the Father? for let him understand well who dares to say, ‘Once the Son was not,’ that he is saying, ‘Once Wisdom was not,’ and ‘Word was not,’ and ‘Life was not.’”

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: And again elsewhere he says:—

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: “But it is not innocent nor without peril, if because of our weakness of understanding we deprive God, as far as in us lies, of the Only-begotten Word ever co-existing with Him; and the Wisdom in which He rejoiced; else He must be conceived as not always possessed of joy.”

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: See, we are proving that this view has been transmitted from father to father; but ye, O modern Jews and disciples of Caiaphas, how many fathers can ye assign to your phrases?

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: Not one of the understanding and wise; for all abhor you, but the devil alone; none but he is your father in this apostasy, who both in the beginning sowed you with the seed of this irreligion, and now persuades you to slander the Ecumenical Council, for committing to writing, not your doctrines, but that which from the beginning those who were eye-witnesses and ministers of the Word have handed down to us.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: For the faith which the Council has confessed in writing, that is the faith of the Catholic Church; to assert this, the blessed Fathers so expressed themselves while condemning the Arian heresy; and this is a chief reason why these apply themselves to calumniate the Council.

verses.book: De Decretis. (Defence of the Nicene Definition.)
verses.text: For it is not the terms which trouble them, but that those terms prove them to be heretics, and presumptuous beyond other heresies.
